chapter_context.summary: A three-year famine is resolved by executing seven descendants of Saul to make amends for Saul's slaughter of the Gibeonites; giant wars are described.

outline.text: Three-Year Famine for Saul's Slaughter of Gibeonites; Redress Demanded
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Three-Year Famine for Saul's Slaughter of Gibeonites; Redress Demanded (21:1-6), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of 2 Samuel.

outline.text: Mephibosheth Spared; Seven Descendants of Saul Executed
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Mephibosheth Spared; Seven Descendants of Saul Executed (21:7-9), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of 2 Samuel.

outline.text: Rizpah's Touching Vigil; David Buries Bones of Saul and Jonathan
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Rizpah's Touching Vigil; David Buries Bones of Saul and Jonathan (21:10-14), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of 2 Samuel.

outline.text: Four Battles with Giants; David's Mighty Men Kill the Giants
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Four Battles with Giants; David's Mighty Men Kill the Giants (21:15-22), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of 2 Samuel.

key_themes.title: The Davidic Covenant
key_themes.description: God's unconditional promise of an eternal dynasty, ultimately pointing forward to the Messiah.

key_themes.title: Sin and Consequences
key_themes.description: While God offers complete forgiveness for sincere repentance, the earthly consequences of sin remain.

devotional.question: How do the events of Chapter 21 demonstrate the grace of God in the midst of human failure?
What lessons about leadership and accountability can you apply to your own life from this chapter?

cultural_historical.title: Jerusalem as Capital
cultural_historical.text: By capturing Jerusalem, a neutral city on the border of Judah and Benjamin, David strategically united the fractured northern and southern tribes under a single capital.
